deliveredOrders.example: 260 delivered out of 500 total orders (52%). Over half of all orders were successfully completed.

deliveredFromConfirmed.description: A donut chart showing delivered orders from confirmed orders as a percentage. This measures the success rate of your confirmation-to-delivery pipeline.
deliveredFromConfirmed.example: 240 delivered from 320 confirmed orders (75%). Three-quarters of confirmed orders made it to successful delivery.

totalAssignments.description: Total number of order assignments distributed across all employees during the selected period. Counts every unique assignment (employee-to-order link).
totalAssignments.example: 350 total assignments — 8 employees shared 350 order handling tasks between them.

confirmedPercent.description: Percentage of assigned orders that employees successfully confirmed (moved to confirmed status) out of all assignments. A higher rate indicates efficient employee follow-up.
confirmedPercent.example: 72% confirmation rate — out of 350 assignments, employees confirmed 252 orders, meaning they successfully contacted customers and verified 252 orders.

cancelledPercent.description: Percentage of assigned orders that were cancelled out of all assignments. A high cancellation rate may indicate issues with order verification or customer responsiveness.
cancelledPercent.example: 8% cancellation rate — 28 out of 350 assignments ended in cancellation, possibly because customers changed their minds or could not be reached.

preparationFailedCount.description: Number of preparation-phase scan failures across all employees. These are cases where an employee attempted to prepare an order but the scan failed (e.g., item not found).
preparationFailedCount.example: 20 preparation failures — employees attempted to prepare 15 orders but encountered scan errors during the warehouse preparation phase.

employeeName.description: The name and avatar of the employee being evaluated. This is the team member whose performance metrics are displayed in the row.
employeeName.example: Ahmed Hassan — the employee whose confirmed, shipped, delivered, and failed counts are shown across the row.

activeAssignments.description: Number of order assignments currently active for this employee.
activeAssignments.example: 12 active assignments — Ahmed currently has 12 orders in his active work queue that he is handling.

receivedOrders.description: Total number of orders assigned to this employee during the selected period. all orders this employee was responsible for.
receivedOrders.example: 45 received orders — Ahmed was assigned 45 orders this month to handle.

confirmedOrders.description: Number of orders that this employee successfully confirmed (moved to confirmed status). These are orders where the employee contacted the customer and verified the order.
confirmedOrders.example: 32 confirmed orders — Ahmed confirmed 32 out of 45 assigned orders by contacting customers and verifying details.

confirmationRate.description: Percentage of assigned orders this employee confirmed. Calculated as (confirmedCount / totalAssigned) * 100. Higher rates indicate more effective customer follow-up.
confirmationRate.example: 71.1% confirmation rate — Ahmed confirmed 32 out of 45 orders, which is a strong confirmation performance.

shippedOrders.description: Number of orders assigned to this employee that reached the SHIPPED status.
shippedOrders.example: 28 shipped orders — 28 of Ahmed's assigned orders were shipped out for delivery.

shippedRate.description: Percentage of assigned orders that were shipped. Calculated as (shippedCount / totalAssigned) * 100. Shows how many of the employee's orders made it to the shipping stage.
shippedRate.example: 62.2% shipped rate — 28 out of 45 orders Ahmed handled were shipped to customers.

deliveredOrders.description: Number of orders assigned to this employee that reached the DELIVERED status. These are successfully completed deliveries from this employee's workload.
deliveredOrders.example: 22 delivered orders — 22 of Ahmed's orders were successfully delivered to customers.

deliveryRate.description: Percentage of assigned orders that were successfully delivered. Calculated as (deliveredCount / totalAssigned) * 100. This is the ultimate success metric for an employee's order handling.
deliveryRate.example: 48.9% delivery rate — 22 out of 45 orders Ahmed handled were successfully delivered. A good delivery rate indicates efficient end-to-end order processing.

preparationFailedCount.description: Number of preparation-phase scan failures for this employee. These are cases where the employee tried to prepare an order but the scan failed during the warehouse phase.
preparationFailedCount.example: 3 preparation failures — Ahmed had 3 failed preparation scans, possibly due to stock issues or incorrect item scanning.

topCity.description: The city with the highest number of delivered shipments across all time. Shows the city name and the count of successfully delivered shipments.
topCity.example: Cairo (450) — Cairo has the most delivered shipments with 450 successful deliveries, making it your top-performing delivery city.

lowestCity.description: The city with the lowest number of delivered shipments. This may indicate areas with fewer orders, delivery challenges, or areas that need more coverage.
lowestCity.example: Luxor (12) — Luxor has only 12 delivered shipments, indicating either low demand or potential delivery issues in that area.

avgDeliveries.description: Overall delivery success rate across all cities. Calculated as (total delivered shipments / total shipments) * 100. This is the global delivery performance metric.
avgDeliveries.example: 87.5% delivery rate — out of all shipments, 87.5% were successfully delivered, indicating strong overall delivery performance.

city.description: The city/area name for the shipment data row. Each row represents aggregated shipment statistics for a specific city.
city.example: Cairo — this row shows all shipment statistics for the Cairo area.

totalShipments.description: Total number of shipments sent to this city during the selected period. Includes all shipment statuses — delivered, failed, and cancelled.
totalShipments.example: 200 total shipments to Cairo — 200 packages were sent to Cairo during this period.

actualDeliveries.description: Number of shipments that were successfully delivered (status = DELIVERED) in this city. This is the count of completed deliveries.
actualDeliveries.example: 175 actual deliveries in Cairo — 175 out of 200 shipments to Cairo were successfully delivered to customers.

failedShipments.description: Number of shipments that failed or were cancelled (status = FAILED or CANCELLED) in this city. These are deliveries that did not reach the customer.
failedShipments.example: 25 failed/cancelled shipments in Cairo — 25 out of 200 shipments to Cairo were not delivered due to failures or cancellations.

successRate.description: Delivery success rate for this city. Calculated as (actualDeliveries / totalShipments) * 100. Color-coded: green for 85%+, orange for 60-84%, red for below 60%.
successRate.example: 87.5% success rate for Cairo — 175 out of 200 shipments were delivered, showing excellent delivery performance for this city.

total.description: The total number of automation executions (excluding cancelled ones). This includes all triggered automation runs across all workflows — pending, running, completed, failed, and paused.
total.example: 1,251 total automation runs — across all automation workflows, 1,251 executions have been triggered by order-related events.

running.description: The number of automation executions currently in progress. These workflows are actively processing their steps right now (sending messages, updating statuses, assigning employees, etc.).
running.example: 8 running executions — 8 automation workflows are currently processing their steps, such as sending WhatsApp templates and updating order statuses.

problems.description: The number of automation executions that failed at any step. A workflow fails when any action encounters an error — such as a WhatsApp message failure, an upsell offer failure, an invalid template, or an incorrect phone number. These executions require review or manual retry.
problems.example: 3 failed executions — 3 automations stopped due to errors (for example, a missing WhatsApp template, an invalid customer phone number, or insufficient stock for an upsell product). Click 'View' to see which step failed and why.

completed.description: The number of automation executions that completed all steps successfully. Every action in the workflow (status updates, WhatsApp messages, employee assignments, etc.) was executed without errors.
completed.example: 1,240 completed executions — 1,240 automations ran from the trigger to the final action without any errors, completing their entire workflow successfully.

pending.description: The number of automation executions waiting in the queue to start. These workflows have been triggered but have not begun processing yet — they are waiting for available resources.
pending.example: 2 pending executions — 2 automations have been triggered and are currently queued, waiting to start processing their steps.

paused.description: The number of automation executions that have been temporarily paused. These workflows started running but were stopped during execution — either manually by a user or due to system conditions. They can be resumed later.
paused.example: 1 paused execution — 1 automation was paused during execution (for example, for review or because of rate limiting) and can be resumed later.

automation.description: The name and version of the automation workflow that was executed. This helps identify exactly which workflow design was used for this execution.
automation.example: Welcome Message v1.2 — this execution ran the 'Welcome Message' automation workflow using version 1.2 of its design.

trigger.description: The event type that triggered this automation execution. Examples include 'order_created', 'order_updated', 'shipment_created', and 'shipment_updated'.
trigger.example: Order Created — this automation execution started when a new order was created in the system.

status.description: The current execution status. Possible values are: pending (queued), running (currently executing), completed (all steps succeeded), failed (stopped because of an error), and paused (temporarily stopped).
status.example: Failed — the automation started running but encountered an error during one of its steps, such as a WhatsApp API error or a failed condition evaluation.

progress.description: Displays execution progress, including the number of completed steps, the current or failed node, and the error message if the workflow failed. For completed executions, it shows the total number of steps completed.
progress.example: 3 steps completed — failed at 'Send WhatsApp Template': 'Template not found'. This execution completed 3 steps before failing because the WhatsApp template was deleted or renamed.

startedAt.description: The date and time when the automation execution was triggered and started processing. This corresponds to the moment the trigger event occurred.
startedAt.example: Started at 2025-01-15 14:30:00 — the automation was triggered on January 15 at 2:30 PM when a new order was created.

actions.description: Action buttons for this execution. Click 'View' to open the full execution details page, where you can see step-by-step execution data, inputs, outputs, timing information, and error details.
actions.example: Click the eye icon to open the execution details page and see which steps succeeded, which failed, and the complete execution path.

automations.description: Automation is a sequence of steps that execute one after another, triggered by a specific event. Each automation starts with a trigger — such as when an order is created, an order status is updated, a shipment is created, or a shipment is updated. Once triggered, the automation runs through its configured steps in order. Each step can depend on the result of the previous step — for example, checking if an order meets certain conditions before sending a WhatsApp message. If a step requires a response (like waiting for a customer reply on WhatsApp), the automation pauses at that step and resumes automatically once the response is received. This allows you to build smart workflows that react to real events and customer interactions without manual intervention.
automations.example: Example: You create an automation triggered on 'order_created'. Step 1 checks if the order total is above $50. If yes, Step 2 sends a WhatsApp confirmation template to the customer. Step 3 waits for the customer to reply. Once the customer responds, Step 4 assigns the order to a specific employee. If the customer doesn't respond within 24 hours, Step 5 sends a follow-up reminder. This entire flow runs automatically without you touching anything.

cities.description: This page allows you to configure shipping settings for each city. You can set the minimum and maximum shipping days, which represent the average delivery timeframe for that city. These values are used to track late shipments. If an order's shipping duration exceeds the maximum days you set for its city, that order is marked as a late shipping order. You can then go to the Shipping tab, filter for late orders, export the list, and share it with the shipping company to investigate delays and improve delivery performance.
cities.example: Example: For Cairo, you set min = 2 days and max = 5 days. This means most orders to Cairo take 2-5 days to deliver. If an order to Cairo is still not delivered after 5 days, it is flagged as 'late'. You go to the Shipping tab, filter by 'Late' status, export those orders, and send the list to your shipping partner asking why they are late.

minDays.description: The minimum expected shipping days for this city. This represents the fastest realistic delivery time based on historical data and is used as a baseline for the expected delivery window.
minDays.example: Example: Cairo has min = 2 days, meaning the fastest orders to Cairo arrive in 2 days.

maxDays.description: The maximum allowed shipping days for this city. If an order takes longer than this to ship, it is automatically marked as a late shipping order. This is the threshold used to identify problematic deliveries.
maxDays.example: Example: Cairo has max = 5 days. If an order to Cairo hasn't shipped after 5 days, it appears in the 'Late Orders' list in the Shipping tab.

status.description: Shows whether shipping configuration (min/max days) has been set for this city. 'Configured' means the city has shipping day limits. 'Not Configured' means the city has no shipping settings yet and orders to this city won't be tracked for late shipping.
status.example: Example: Cairo shows 'Configured' because you set min=2 and max=5. Alexandria shows 'Not Configured' because you haven't set shipping days for it yet.

type.description: Shows whether a role is Global or Custom. Global roles come pre-configured from the system and cannot be edited or deleted — they provide standard permission sets that are always available. Custom roles are created by you with specific permissions, allowing you to give your employees exactly the access they need.
type.example: Example: 'Super Admin' is a Global role with full system access that you cannot modify. 'Warehouse Manager' is a Custom role you created with only warehouse-related permissions like order.view and shipment.update.

totalPurchases.description: The total value of all purchases recorded from all suppliers in your system. This represents your complete purchase history with suppliers and increases whenever new purchases are added. It is a historical total and is not reduced when payments are made.
totalPurchases.example: Example: If you bought $15,000 from Supplier A, $8,500 from Supplier B, and $22,000 from Supplier C, the total purchases shown will be $45,500.

totalDue.description: The current net balance between your business and all suppliers. A positive amount means your business still needs to pay suppliers. A negative amount means suppliers owe money back to your business, such as from returns, credits, or overpayments.
totalDue.example: Example: If you owe Supplier A $3,200, have a $500 credit with Supplier B, and owe Supplier C $7,800, the net amount shown will be $10,500.

categories.description: The product categories associated with this supplier, displayed as tags. Categories help you organize suppliers by the types of products they provide, such as Electronics, Clothing, or Food. A supplier can belong to multiple categories.
categories.example: Example: A supplier may be listed under Electronics, Accessories, and Mobile Parts.

dueBalance.description: The current balance with this supplier. If the amount is positive, your business still needs to pay the supplier. If the amount is negative, the supplier owes money back to your business because of credits, returns, or overpayments.
dueBalance.example: Example: If you bought goods worth $10,000 and have already paid $6,500, the remaining balance is $3,500. If the balance becomes -$200, the supplier owes your business $200.

purchaseValue.description: The total amount your business has purchased from this supplier since they were added to the system. This is your complete purchase history with the supplier and increases whenever a new purchase is recorded.
purchaseValue.example: Example: If you made purchases of $2,000, $5,500, and $3,000 from the same supplier, the total shown will be $10,500.

pendingBalance.description: The current balance between your business and this supplier after all purchases, returns, refunds, credits, and payments have been calculated. A positive balance means your business still owes money to the supplier. A negative balance means the supplier owes money back to your business, such as from product returns, credit notes, or overpayments.
pendingBalance.example: Example: If your purchases total $10,000 and your payments total $6,500, the balance is $3,500, meaning you still owe the supplier $3,500. If the balance becomes -$200, the supplier owes your business $200.

totalPurchases.description: The total value of all purchases you have made from this supplier. This is your complete buying history with them — every purchase invoice is added to this total. It increases whenever you make a new purchase and is not reduced by payments.
totalPurchases.example: Example: You placed orders of $2,000, $5,500, and $3,000 with this supplier over time. The total purchases shown will be $10,500.

purchases.description: Shows all purchase invoices for this supplier. Each row represents a purchase you made from the supplier, including the invoice date, total amount, how much was paid, and what remains. You can filter by date range to find specific purchases and click to view full invoice details.
purchases.example: Example: You want to check all purchases you made from Supplier X in January. You open the Purchases tab, set the date range to January 1-31, and see a list of all invoices with their amounts and payment status.

returns.description: Shows all return invoices related to this supplier. When you return products to a supplier (damaged, wrong items, etc.), the return is recorded here. Each row shows the return date, total return amount, how much the supplier refunded, and the status of the return (accepted, pending, or rejected).
returns.example: Example: You returned $800 worth of defective products to Supplier Y. The Returns tab shows this return with status 'Accepted' and confirms the $800 credit has been applied to your balance.

history.description: Shows all closed purchase and return periods for this supplier. When you close a period, all purchase invoices and return invoices within that timeframe are finalized and locked from further changes. You can select a year, review the closed periods, and see the total purchases, returns, and final balance for each period.
history.example: Example: You close the period for Q1 2024 (January-March) for Supplier Z. The History tab shows this period was closed on March 31, 2024, with $25,000 in purchases, $2,000 in returns, and a final balance of $23,000.

payments.description: Shows all payments recorded for this supplier. Each payment record includes the amount paid, the payment date, whether the payment was made for a specific invoice or as a general payment toward the supplier's balance, and any notes added during the payment process.
payments.example: Example: You recorded a payment of $5,000 to Supplier A on March 15 via bank transfer and linked it to Purchase Invoice #INV-1024. The Payments tab shows the amount, date, the invoice it was paid for, and any notes entered with the payment.

receiptNumber.description: A unique identifier assigned to each purchase invoice. It is automatically generated when a new purchase is created and is used to track and reference the invoice throughout the system.
receiptNumber.example: Example: If this is the first purchase created, the receipt number might be PUR-001. The next purchase would be PUR-002, and so on.

supplier.description: The name of the supplier from whom the products were purchased. If a supplier was not selected during purchase creation, this field will display a dash (-). The supplier determines who the business owes payment to.
supplier.example: Example: If you purchased 50 units of Phone Cases from 'Tech Supplies Co.', the supplier column will show 'Tech Supplies Co.'.

date.description: The date when the purchase invoice was created. This is the date the purchase was recorded in the system, which may differ from the actual delivery or payment date.
date.example: Example: If you created a purchase invoice on March 15, 2024, the date column will show '3/15/2024'.

subtotal.description: The sum of all item line totals in the purchase invoice before any additional adjustments. This is calculated by multiplying each item's quantity by its purchase cost and summing all line totals.
subtotal.example: Example: If you bought 10 units of Product A at $20 each (line total $200) and 5 units of Product B at $30 each (line total $150), the subtotal will be $350.

paidAmount.description: The amount that has been paid to the supplier at the time the purchase was created. If no payment was made, this will be $0. This amount is deducted from the total to calculate the remaining balance.
paidAmount.example: Example: If the purchase total is $1,000 and you paid $400 upfront, the paid amount column will show $400 in green.

remainingAmount.description: The amount still owed to the supplier for this purchase. It is calculated as the purchase total minus the paid amount. A positive value means you still owe the supplier. A zero value means the purchase is fully paid.
remainingAmount.example: Example: If the purchase total is $1,000 and you paid $400, the remaining amount will be $600 shown in orange. If you paid the full $1,000, it will show $0 in gray.

products.description: A list of all items included in this purchase invoice. Each item shows the product name, SKU (stock keeping unit), quantity purchased, and the purchase cost per unit. This gives you a quick overview of what was purchased.
products.example: Example: 'iPhone Case - IC-001 - (x10) (x$5.00 SYP)' means 10 units of iPhone Case (SKU: IC-001) were purchased at $5.00 each.

safe.description: The cash safe or payment account used to fund this purchase. This indicates which of your financial accounts the payment was made from or will be made from.
safe.example: Example: If you paid from your 'Main Cash Safe', the safe column will show 'Main Cash Safe' with a wallet icon.

receipt.description: An uploaded image or document of the physical receipt or invoice from the supplier. This serves as proof of the purchase and can be previewed by clicking on it.
receipt.example: Example: A scanned copy of the supplier's printed invoice showing the items purchased, quantities, and prices.

status.description: The current status of the purchase invoice. Statuses include: Pending (not yet reviewed), Accepted (approved and confirmed), Rejected (denied), and Draft (saved but not submitted).
status.example: Example: A new purchase starts as 'Pending'. After review, it can be 'Accepted' (green), 'Rejected' (red), or remain 'Draft' (gray).

actions.description: Row-level actions for managing this purchase invoice. Includes viewing details, checking audit logs, accepting or rejecting the invoice, and changing status. Available actions depend on the current status of the invoice.
actions.example: Example: Click the three-dot menu on a pending purchase to accept it (green check), reject it (red X), or view the full invoice details.

returnNumber.description: A unique identifier assigned to each purchase return invoice. It is automatically generated when a new return is created and is used to track and reference the return throughout the system.
returnNumber.example: Example: The first return invoice might be PRN-001. The next return would be PRN-002, and so on.

invoiceNumber.description: The receipt number of the original purchase invoice that this return references. This links the return back to the specific purchase from which the products are being returned.
invoiceNumber.example: Example: If you are returning items from purchase PUR-015, the invoice number column will show 'PUR-015'.

returnDate.description: The date when the purchase return invoice was created. This is the date the return was recorded in the system.
returnDate.example: Example: If you created a return on April 5, 2024, the return date column will show '4/5/2024'.

createdBy.description: The name of the user who created this purchase return invoice. This tracks accountability for who initiated the return in the system.
createdBy.example: Example: If employee 'Ahmed Hassan' created the return, this column will display 'Ahmed Hassan'.

supplierName.description: A snapshot of the supplier name at the time the return was created. This is captured from the original purchase invoice and saved separately so the return record retains the correct supplier name even if the supplier's details change later.
supplierName.example: Example: If the original purchase was from 'Tech Supplies Co.', the supplier name column will show 'Tech Supplies Co.' even if the supplier was renamed later.

subtotal.description: The sum of all returned item line subtotals before tax. This is calculated by multiplying each returned item's quantity by its unit cost and summing all line subtotals.
subtotal.example: Example: If you returned 5 units of Product A at $10 each (line subtotal $50) and 3 units of Product B at $20 each (line subtotal $60), the subtotal will be $110.

tax.description: The total tax amount applied to all returned items. Tax is calculated per item based on whether the item is tax-inclusive and its tax rate. This shows the total tax being refunded.
tax.example: Example: If Product A has a 10% tax rate on a $100 subtotal ($10 tax) and Product B has a 5% tax rate on a $60 subtotal ($3 tax), the tax total will be $13.

totalReturn.description: The total value of this return invoice, calculated as the subtotal plus the tax total. This is the full amount the supplier should refund or credit back to your business.
totalReturn.example: Example: If the subtotal is $110 and the tax is $13, the total return will be $123. This is the total amount the supplier owes you for this return.

safe.description: The cash safe or payment account associated with this return. This indicates which financial account is affected by the return transaction.
safe.example: Example: If the return refund is recorded against your 'Main Cash Safe', this column will show 'Main Cash Safe' with a wallet icon.

paidAmount.description: The amount the supplier has already refunded or credited for this return. If no refund has been received yet, this will be $0.
paidAmount.example: Example: If the total return is $123 and the supplier refunded $80 so far, the paid amount will show $80 in green.

remainingAmount.description: The amount still owed by the supplier for this return. It is calculated as totalReturn minus paidAmount. A positive value means the supplier still owes you money. A zero value means the return is fully settled.
remainingAmount.example: Example: If the total return is $123 and you received an $80 refund, the remaining amount will be $43 shown in orange. If the full amount was refunded, it shows $0 in gray.

products.description: A list of all items included in this return invoice. Each item shows the product name, SKU, returned quantity, unit cost, and tax rate. This gives you a quick overview of what was returned.
products.example: Example: 'iPhone Case - IC-001 - (x3) (x$5.00 SYP) (Tax 10%)' means 3 units of iPhone Case were returned at $5.00 each with 10% tax.

receipt.description: An uploaded image or document of the return receipt or credit note from the supplier. This serves as proof of the return transaction and can be previewed by clicking on it.
receipt.example: Example: A photo of the supplier's signed return acknowledgment or credit note showing the returned items and refund amount.

status.description: The current status of the purchase return invoice. Common statuses include Accepted, Pending, and Rejected. The status reflects whether the return has been approved and processed by the supplier.
status.example: Example: A return starts as 'Pending'. After the supplier processes it, it becomes 'Accepted' (green) or 'Rejected' (red).

actions.description: Row-level actions for managing this purchase return invoice. Includes viewing details, checking audit logs, and editing the paid amount. The edit paid amount action is disabled if the return is linked to a closed period.
actions.example: Example: Click the three-dot menu on a return to view full details, check logs, or update the refund amount received from the supplier.

general.description: The General tab controls core order processing behavior: duplicate detection, automatic order assignment to employees, the master retry system toggle, and retry limits (how many times and how often the system retries contacting customers).
general.example: Example: Set max retries to 3 with a 30-minute interval, enable auto-assignment in 'Immediate' mode, and configure a 24-hour duplicate detection window to prevent the same customer from placing duplicate orders.

automation.description: The Automation tab defines what happens when the retry system finishes contacting a customer. It controls which status the order moves to after exhausting retries, which order statuses trigger automatic retries, and which statuses count as successful outcomes that stop the retry cycle.
automation.example: Example: Set retry statuses to 'Pending Confirmation' and 'New', set confirmation statuses to 'Confirmed' and 'Paid', and auto-move unresolved orders to 'No Response' after 3 attempts.

shipping.description: The Shipping tab controls the order flow path (whether confirmed orders go to Warehouse first or directly to Shipping) and stock management settings (when inventory is deducted and whether reserved stock is enabled).
shipping.example: Example: Choose 'Direct to Shipping' to skip the warehouse step, set stock deduction to 'On Confirmation', and enable reserved stock to prevent overselling.

notifications.description: The Notifications tab controls whether the admin receives a notification when all retry attempts end with no customer response, helping management follow up on delayed or unresolved orders.
notifications.example: Example: Enable 'Notify Admin' so the business owner gets alerted when an order has gone through all retry attempts without a customer response.

sync.description: The Store Sync tab manages integration with connected e-commerce stores (Shopify, WooCommerce, EasyOrder). It controls automatic product synchronization per store and a SKU fallback option for matching incoming order items to local products when no direct sync link exists.
sync.example: Example: Enable 'Sync New Products' for your Shopify store so new products are automatically imported, and enable SKU fallback so orders from WooCommerce match local variants by SKU even without a sync mapping.

duplicateWindow.description: The time window (in hours) the system looks back to detect duplicate orders. Any order with the same phone number and items placed within this window is flagged as a potential duplicate.
duplicateWindow.example: Example: Set to 24 hours — if the same phone number places two orders with identical items within 24 hours, the second order is flagged as a duplicate.

autoCancelDuplicates.description: When enabled, detected duplicate orders are automatically marked as 'DUPLICATE' status without manual review. When disabled, duplicates are flagged but still require manual action.
autoCancelDuplicates.example: Example: Enable this so the system automatically cancels duplicate orders, saving your team from manually reviewing and rejecting them.

assignmentMode.description: Controls how new orders are assigned to employees. 'Disabled' means orders remain unassigned until manually distributed. 'Immediate' applies automation rules and assigns orders as soon as they arrive. 'Delayed' waits for a configurable period before assigning, giving time for automatic WhatsApp confirmation first.
assignmentMode.example: Example: Choose 'Delayed' with a 30-minute wait so orders can be confirmed via WhatsApp before being assigned to the call center.

enableRetry.description: Enables or disables the automatic retry workflow. When enabled, if an employee contacts a customer but does not change the order to a successful status, the system will schedule another follow-up attempt according to the configured retry settings. When disabled, employees can still contact customers manually, but the system will not schedule automatic retries.
enableRetry.example: Example: Enable this so if an employee calls a customer and the order remains in a retry status (such as 'Pending Confirmation'), the system automatically schedules another follow-up until the maximum number of attempts is reached.

maxRetries.description: The maximum number of times the system will attempt to contact the customer before stopping and optionally moving the order to a different status. Higher values mean more contact attempts.
maxRetries.example: Example: Set to 3 — the system will try to reach the customer 3 times. If all 3 attempts fail, the order moves to the auto-move status (e.g., 'No Response').

retryInterval.description: The number of minutes the system waits between each retry attempt. This gives the customer time to respond or call back before the next attempt is made.
retryInterval.example: Example: Set to 30 minutes — if the first attempt fails at 10:00 AM, the second attempt happens at 10:30 AM, and the third at 11:00 AM.

autoMoveStatus.description: The status that orders are automatically moved to after all retry attempts are exhausted without a customer response. This helps route unresolved orders to a queue for manual follow-up.
autoMoveStatus.example: Example: Choose 'No Response' — after 3 failed attempts, the order moves to 'No Response' status where your team can see it needs special attention.

retryStatuses.description: Defines the order statuses that keep an order in the automatic retry workflow. If, after contacting the customer, the employee changes the order to one of these statuses, the system schedules another follow-up attempt. Orders moved to any other status are excluded from the retry workflow unless they return to one of these statuses.
retryStatuses.example: Example: Select 'New' and 'Pending Confirmation'. If an employee finishes a call and leaves the order as 'Pending Confirmation', the system schedules another retry. If the employee changes it to 'Confirmed', no retry is scheduled.

confirmationStatuses.description: Defines the order statuses that are considered successful outcomes. When an employee changes the order to one of these statuses after contacting the customer, the order is treated as resolved and the automatic retry workflow stops immediately.
confirmationStatuses.example: Example: Select 'Confirmed' and 'Paid'. If an employee confirms the order with the customer and changes its status to 'Confirmed', the system cancels any remaining retry attempts.

orderFlowPath.description: Determines where confirmed orders go next. 'Warehouse' routes orders to the Warehouse tab for packing and preparation before shipping. 'Shipping' bypasses the warehouse and sends orders directly to the shipping company for delivery.
orderFlowPath.example: Example: Choose 'Warehouse' if your business needs to pick, pack, and prepare items before shipping. Choose 'Shipping' if items are ready to ship immediately after confirmation.

stockDeductionStrategy.description: Controls when inventory is deducted from available stock. 'On Confirmation' deducts stock as soon as the order is confirmed. 'Upon Shipment' delays deduction until the order is actually shipped, keeping stock available for potential changes.
stockDeductionStrategy.example: Example: Choose 'On Confirmation' to reserve inventory immediately when a customer confirms, preventing overselling. Choose 'Upon Shipment' if orders frequently change before shipping.

reservedEnabled.description: When enabled, items in new orders are virtually reserved and subtracted from 'Available Stock' to prevent over-selling. This is a virtual hold, not a physical deduction from 'Stock on Hand'. Reserved stock is automatically released if the order is canceled or returned.
reservedEnabled.example: Example: Enable this so when an order with 5 units of Product A is placed, those 5 units are removed from available stock even before the order is confirmed or shipped.

notifyAdmin.description: When enabled, the admin receives a notification when all retry attempts for an order end with no customer response. This helps management stay informed about unresolved orders that need follow-up.
notifyAdmin.example: Example: Enable this so when an order goes through 3 retry attempts without a response, the admin gets a notification saying 'Order #12345 needs follow-up — all attempts failed'.

skuFallback.description: When enabled, if an order arrives from a connected store and no direct product sync mapping exists, the system attempts to match the order item to a local product variant by SKU code before moving the order to pending.
skuFallback.example: Example: A WooCommerce order arrives with SKU 'IPH-15-BLK'. No sync link exists for this product, but a local variant with SKU 'IPH-15-BLK' is found — the order item is matched automatically.
